title_en: Formation of the Legal Schools: Historical Analysis
content_en:
One of the central concerns of Al-Fikr as-Sami is explaining why and how the four major Sunni legal schools — the Hanafi, Maliki, Shafi'i, and Hanbali madhabs — emerged from the common foundation of Quran and Sunnah that all Muslims share. Al-Hajwi's analysis of this question draws on both the traditional biographical literature and his own historical perspective.

He traced the formation of distinct legal schools to several converging factors: regional variation in the hadith traditions available to early scholars (Madinah, Kufa, Basra, and Syria had access to different collections of Companion and Successor opinions); differences in methodological approach to the relationship between text and reason; and the personal intellectual characteristics of the founding imams — Abu Hanifa's systematic rationalism, Malik's adherence to the practice of Madinah, Al-Shafi'i's synthesis of hadith and systematic legal theory, and Ahmad ibn Hanbal's strict traditionalism.

Al-Hajwi argued that the consolidation of fiqh into four recognized schools was a historically contingent process that took several centuries and involved factors of political patronage, institutional development, and educational reproduction as well as purely intellectual factors. The Abbasid caliphate's eventual recognition of the four schools as the authorized forms of Sunni legal interpretation gave the process its definitive shape.

His analysis of the competing legal methodologies — particularly the tension between ahl ar-ra'y (scholars who prioritized systematic reasoning) and ahl al-hadith (scholars who prioritized close adherence to transmitted reports) — provides a historical framework for understanding why the different schools took the positions they did on disputed questions.

For the Maliki school in particular — his own tradition — al-Hajwi's analysis is richest and most detailed, drawing on the specific sources and scholarly networks of the Maghreb tradition that he knew from the inside. His ability to speak from within a tradition while still analyzing it historically gave Al-Fikr as-Sami a combination of insider depth and critical distance that works written from purely external perspectives cannot achieve. This quality has made the work valuable both to those seeking to understand Islamic jurisprudence as a living tradition and to those approaching it as a subject of historical study.
content_id:
Salah satu perhatian sentral Al-Fikr as-Sami adalah menjelaskan mengapa dan bagaimana empat mazhab hukum Sunni utama — Hanafi, Maliki, Syafi'i, dan Hanbali — muncul dari fondasi bersama Al-Quran dan Sunnah yang dimiliki oleh semua kaum Muslim. Analisis Al-Hajwi atas pertanyaan ini mengacu pada literatur biografi tradisional maupun perspektif historisnya sendiri.

Ia menelusuri pembentukan mazhab-mazhab hukum yang berbeda pada beberapa faktor yang berkonvergen: variasi regional dalam tradisi hadis yang tersedia bagi ulama-ulama awal (Madinah, Kufah, Bashrah, dan Syam memiliki akses ke koleksi-koleksi pendapat Sahabat dan Tabi'in yang berbeda); perbedaan dalam pendekatan metodologis terhadap hubungan antara teks dan akal; serta karakteristik intelektual pribadi para imam pendiri — rasionalisme sistematis Abu Hanifah, kepatuhan Malik terhadap praktik Madinah, sintesis al-Syafi'i antara hadis dan teori hukum sistematis, serta tradisionalisme ketat Ahmad ibn Hanbal.

Al-Hajwi berpendapat bahwa konsolidasi fikih ke dalam empat mazhab yang diakui adalah proses yang secara historis bersifat kontingen yang berlangsung beberapa abad dan melibatkan faktor-faktor patronase politik, pengembangan institusional, dan reproduksi pendidikan di samping faktor-faktor intelektual murni. Pengakuan kekhalifahan Abbasiyah akhirnya atas empat mazhab tersebut sebagai bentuk-bentuk interpretasi hukum Sunni yang terotorisasi memberikan proses tersebut bentuk definitifnya.

Analisanya tentang metodologi-metodologi hukum yang bersaing — khususnya ketegangan antara ahl ar-ra'y (ulama yang mengutamakan penalaran sistematis) dan ahl al-hadis (ulama yang mengutamakan kepatuhan ketat terhadap riwayat-riwayat yang ditransmisikan) — memberikan kerangka historis untuk memahami mengapa mazhab-mazhab yang berbeda mengambil posisi yang mereka ambil atas pertanyaan-pertanyaan yang diperdebatkan.

Untuk mazhab Maliki khususnya — tradisinya sendiri — analisis al-Hajwi paling kaya dan paling terperinci, menggali sumber-sumber spesifik dan jaringan-jaringan ulama dari tradisi Maghreb yang ia kenal dari dalam. Kemampuannya berbicara dari dalam sebuah tradisi sambil tetap menganalisisnya secara historis memberi Al-Fikr as-Sami kombinasi kedalaman orang dalam dan jarak kritis yang tidak dapat dicapai oleh karya-karya yang ditulis dari perspektif eksternal murni. Kualitas ini telah membuat karya tersebut berharga baik bagi mereka yang ingin memahami yurisprudensi Islam sebagai tradisi yang hidup maupun bagi mereka yang mendekatinya sebagai subjek kajian historis.
content_ar: One of the المحوري concerns of Al-Fikr as-Sami is explaining why and how the four major Sunni legal schools — the الحنفي, المالكي, الشافعي, and الحنبلي madhabs — emerged from the common foundation of القرآن الكريم and السنة that all المسلمون share. Al-Hajwi's analysis of this question draws on both the التقليدي biographical literature and his own historical perspective. He traced the formation of distinct legal schools to several converging factors: regional variation in the الحديث traditions available to early العلماء (المدينة المنورة, Kufa, Basra, and Syria had access to different collections of Companion and Successor opinions); differences in methodological approach to the relationship between text and العقل; and the personal intellectual characteristics of the founding imams — Abu Hanifa's systematic rationalism, مالك's adherence to the practice of المدينة المنورة, Al-الشافعي's synthesis of الحديث and systematic legal theory, and أحمد ابن حنبل's strict traditionalism. Al-Hajwi argued that the consolidation of الفقه into four recognized schools was a historically contingent process that took several centuries and involved factors of political patronage, institutional development, and educational reproduction as well as purely intellectual factors. The Abbasid الخلافة's eventual recognition of the four schools as the authorized forms of Sunni legal interpretation gave the process its definitive shape. His analysis of the competing legal methodologies — particularly the tension between ahl ar-ra'y (العلماء who prioritized systematic reasoning) and ahl al-الحديث (العلماء who prioritized close adherence to transmitted reports) — provides a historical framework for الفهم why the different schools took the positions they did on disputed questions. For the المالكي school وبالأخص — his own التراث — al-Hajwi's analysis is richest and most detailed, drawing on the specific sources and العلمي networks of the Maghreb التراث that he knew from the inside. His ability to speak from within a التراث بينما still analyzing it historically gave Al-Fikr as-Sami a combination of insider depth and البالغ الأهمية distance that works written from purely external perspectives cannot achieve. This quality has made the work valuable both to those seeking لفهم Islamic jurisprudence as a living التراث and to those approaching it as a subject of historical study.
